title: Bell's Palsy
oneLiner: Acute, idiopathic, unilateral lower motor neuron facial palsy; most cases resolve spontaneously but steroids improve outcomes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Stroke (CVA) – especially brainstem stroke or lateral medullary syndrome:** *Facial weakness spares forehead (upper motor neuron), but also look for arm/leg drift, dysarthria, visual field loss, Horner’s, ataxia.* If the forehead is moving – not Bell’s palsy. Also check NIHSS or FAST.
- **Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster reactivation):** *Same lower motor neuron pattern but with vesicular rash in the ear canal, on the pinna, or on the palate. Can also cause severe pain, hearing loss, vertigo.* The rash is the killer clue – miss it and you give steroids alone, which can worsen viral spread.
- **Lyme neuroborreliosis (especially in endemic areas):** *Often bilateral facial palsy, history of tick bite, erythema migrans rash, or other neurological symptoms (meningitis, radiculopathy).* Unilateral Lyme palsy is possible but look for bilateral or systemic features.
- **Guillain–Barré syndrome (Miller Fisher variant):** *Look for areflexia, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia.* Facial palsy is often bilateral and accompanied by limb weakness or sensory symptoms.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset (<72 hours) of unilateral facial weakness
  - Inability to close the eye, raise the eyebrow, smile, or pucker lips on the affected side
  - Drooping of the corner of the mouth
  - Often precedes with retroauricular pain (hours to days)
  - Some complain of altered taste (anterior 2/3), hyperacusis, or reduced tearing (because of efferent parasympathetic involvement)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Lower motor neurone pattern:** forehead muscles are affected (cannot raise eyebrow on that side)
  - No other cranial nerve deficits (no VI or VIII palsy) → if present, think alternative
  - No limb weakness, no sensory loss, no ataxia
  - Assess for vesicles in ear, palate, or external auditory canal (Ramsay Hunt)
  - Check for Bell’s phenomenon (upward turning of the eye on attempted closure) — not diagnostic but confirms eye is at risk
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Bell’s palsy (most common; isolated LMN facial palsy, acute onset)
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (vesicles + facial palsy ± other CN involvement)
  - Stroke/TIA (UMN pattern, usually spares forehead, but can be LMN if brainstem – check long tracts)
  - Lyme neuroborreliosis (bilateral palsy, erythema migrans, tick exposure)
  - Guillain–Barré / Miller Fisher (bilateral, areflexia, ophthalmoplegia)
  - Otitis media / mastoiditis (ear pain, fever, tympanic membrane changes)
  - Parotid gland tumour (gradual onset, mass, facial nerve palsy persisting)
  - Sarcoidosis / other granulomatous disease (bilateral, other systemic features)
- **Splitter:** *Isolated forehead involvement (unilateral)?* In Bell’s palsy, forehead is always weak. In UMN stroke, forehead is spared. So if you see facial weakness *with forehead spared*, that is an UMN lesion and is NOT Bell’s palsy – immediate CT head and stroke call.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - FAST / NIHSS stroke screen
  - Otoscopy (check for vesicles, redness, bulging tympanic membrane)
  - Assess eye closure and corneal reflex (if absent, high risk of exposure keratopathy)
  - Pure tone audiometry (if hearing loss or vertigo)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR, U&Es, LFTs, glucose (rule out infection, diabetes as possible precipitant)
  - Serology for Lyme (ELISA + confirmatory Western blot) if unexplained bilateral palsy or tick exposure
  - Varicella-zoster IgG/IgM if suspect Ramsay Hunt (or PCR of vesicle fluid)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None routinely if typical Bell’s palsy (isolated, acute, LMN, no other signs)
  - **Second-line:** CT or MRI head with contrast if atypical features (slow onset, recurrent, other CN involvement, bilateral, no recovery after 4 months) — to exclude mass, meningeal disease, brainstem lesion
- **Special tests:**
  - Electromyography/nerve conduction studies (only if poor recovery or to prognosticate — rarely done acutely)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: no widely accepted scoring system. NICE CKS defines Bell’s palsy as acute (within 72 hours) unilateral LMN facial palsy with no other neurological signs and no identifiable cause.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **FAST / NIHSS stroke screen** – because missing a brainstem stroke (even without limb symptoms) can be catastrophic. If any UMN sign, positive FAST, or facial weakness spares the forehead → head CT.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess eye closure: if incomplete, apply lubricating eye drops (hypromellose 0.3% qds) and eye pad/taping at night to prevent corneal abrasion.
  - Consider if Lyme or Ramsay Hunt is likely (bilateral, rash, tick exposure) – do not start steroids without first ruling out herpes or Lyme (or if high suspicion, add antiviral).
  - Prescribe prednisolone as soon as diagnosis is certain (within 72 hours of onset).
  - Provide patient information leaflet on Bell’s palsy and eye care.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually not admitted unless severe eye involvement, severe pain, unable to eat/drink, or diagnostic uncertainty.
  - If admitted: ensure eye care (lubrication, taping) 4-hourly. Observations NEWS2.
  - Monitor for pain or progression (if worsens or bilateral, reassess for Guillain–Barré).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue prednisolone for 10 days.
  - Eye protection throughout recovery (artificial tears, nocturnal ointment, taping, moisture chamber glasses – consider ophthalmology referral if persistent corneal exposure).
  - Facial exercises (encourage gentle massage and symmetrical movements once acute phase passes) – speech and language therapy referral for severe cases.
  - Follow-up in 2–4 weeks with GP or neurology to assess recovery.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline if nil by mouth, but most patients can swallow. Encourage oral fluids.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely needed (low risk) unless additional immobility.
  - **Diet/NBM:** Normal oral diet if safe swallow; if drooling or dysphagia consider speech and language therapy review.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 q4h if admitted; otherwise, no vital signs required for outpatient.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (for confirmed Bell’s palsy):**
  - **Prednisolone** 50 mg orally once daily for 10 days (or 25 mg twice daily) – start within 72 hours of symptom onset for maximal benefit. [BNF: 50 mg once daily x10 days]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Aciclovir** 800 mg orally 5 times daily for 7 days – add only if Ramsay Hunt syndrome confirmed or suspected (vesicles present) or in severe Bell’s palsy with high suspicion (some clinicians consider adding aciclovir if severe, but NICE does not routinely recommend). Not used alone without steroids.
  - For pregnant women: prednisolone is safe; aciclovir is safe. (Acyclovir for Ramsay Hunt)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Prednisolone: caution in diabetes (monitor glucose), hypertension, active peptic ulcer, severe infection (e.g., herpes simplex keratitis – avoid if ocular HSV suspected)
  - Aciclovir: reduce dose in renal impairment; caution with nephrotoxic drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir dose adjustment in CKD (eGFR <25 mL/min): reduce to 800 mg 3 times daily or [check local policy]
  - Prednisolone: no routine adjustment; may increase risk of fluid retention in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving steroids without ruling out Ramsay Hunt or herpes zoster** – can worsen disseminated VZV. Always examine ear and palate for vesicles before starting prednisolone alone. If vesicles present, add aciclovir.
  - **Omission of eye care** – not a drug error but more harmful: if you discharge without prescribing lubricating eye drops or covering the eye at night, the patient can develop corneal ulceration and permanent vision loss.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal abrasion / exposure keratitis / ulceration (from incomplete eye closure)
  - Syncope or aspiration (rare)
  - Pain
- **Chronic:**
  - Synkinesis (involuntary facial movements – e.g., eye closing when smiling) – occurs in ~10–15% after recovery
  - Facial contracture (long-standing muscle tonicity)
  - Persistent facial weakness (caused by severe degeneration; see prognosis)
  - Psychosocial effects (low mood, social withdrawal)
sections.Prognosis: - 70–85% recover fully (within 3–6 months). Of the rest, ~15% have mild residual weakness, <5% have severe permanent palsy. Prognosis worse if: complete palsy on presentation, severe pain, older age, hypertension, diabetes, or no improvement within 3 weeks. If recovery starts within 3 weeks, outlook is excellent.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a thorough ear exam** (otorrhea, vesicles, bulging tympanic membrane) – you will not miss Ramsay Hunt or otitis media.
- **Always prescribe eye protection for the first 2 weeks** even if the patient has partial closure – give hypromellose drops 4-hourly and Lacri-Lube ointment at night with tape or patch. Document in notes.
- **Tell the patient to return if they cannot close the eye at all** or develop new neurological symptoms (headache, limb weakness, double vision) – stroke can still be missed initially.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not label facial weakness as Bell’s palsy if forehead is spared** – this is an UMN pattern, treat as a stroke until proven otherwise. Do not discharge without CT head.
- **Do not start steroids if you suspect varicella-zoster** – wait for vesicle examination. If rash present, start aciclovir and prednisolone together (but never steroids alone for Ramsay Hunt).
- **Do not reassure that recovery is guaranteed in 2 weeks** – some take months; warn about synkinesis and need for follow-up if no improvement by 3–4 weeks.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Eye pain, redness, or visual disturbance (corneal ulcer)
  - New facial weakness on opposite side (bilateral suggests Guillain–Barré or Lyme)
  - Severe headache, double vision, or arm/leg weakness (possible stroke)
  - Worsening weakness or failure to improve by 3–4 weeks
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP at 2–4 weeks; if no improvement by 4 weeks, refer to neurology
  - Eye clinic if persistent corneal exposure or risk of ulceration
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior or neurology (on-call) immediately if:**
  - Patient has bilateral facial palsy (suspect Guillain–Barré, Lyme, or sarcoid)
  - Any UMN signs (forehead sparing, hemiparesis, visual field loss)
  - Fever, headache, meningism (consider meningitis or brainstem encephalitis)
  - Rapidly progressive weakness or respiratory muscle involvement (Guillain–Barré — monitor spirometry)
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising (e.g., respiratory distress from aspiration or Guillain–Barré)
  - GCS ≤13
  - Seizure
  - Suspected stroke with evidence of brainstem dysfunction (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular breathing) → airway support before CT

rawMarkdown:
## Bell's Palsy

**One-liner:** Acute, idiopathic, unilateral lower motor neuron facial palsy; most cases resolve spontaneously but steroids improve outcomes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Stroke (CVA) – especially brainstem stroke or lateral medullary syndrome:** *Facial weakness spares forehead (upper motor neuron), but also look for arm/leg drift, dysarthria, visual field loss, Horner’s, ataxia.* If the forehead is moving – not Bell’s palsy. Also check NIHSS or FAST.
- **Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster reactivation):** *Same lower motor neuron pattern but with vesicular rash in the ear canal, on the pinna, or on the palate. Can also cause severe pain, hearing loss, vertigo.* The rash is the killer clue – miss it and you give steroids alone, which can worsen viral spread.
- **Lyme neuroborreliosis (especially in endemic areas):** *Often bilateral facial palsy, history of tick bite, erythema migrans rash, or other neurological symptoms (meningitis, radiculopathy).* Unilateral Lyme palsy is possible but look for bilateral or systemic features.
- **Guillain–Barré syndrome (Miller Fisher variant):** *Look for areflexia, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia.* Facial palsy is often bilateral and accompanied by limb weakness or sensory symptoms.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset (<72 hours) of unilateral facial weakness
  - Inability to close the eye, raise the eyebrow, smile, or pucker lips on the affected side
  - Drooping of the corner of the mouth
  - Often precedes with retroauricular pain (hours to days)
  - Some complain of altered taste (anterior 2/3), hyperacusis, or reduced tearing (because of efferent parasympathetic involvement)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Lower motor neurone pattern:** forehead muscles are affected (cannot raise eyebrow on that side)
  - No other cranial nerve deficits (no VI or VIII palsy) → if present, think alternative
  - No limb weakness, no sensory loss, no ataxia
  - Assess for vesicles in ear, palate, or external auditory canal (Ramsay Hunt)
  - Check for Bell’s phenomenon (upward turning of the eye on attempted closure) — not diagnostic but confirms eye is at risk

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Bell’s palsy (most common; isolated LMN facial palsy, acute onset)
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (vesicles + facial palsy ± other CN involvement)
  - Stroke/TIA (UMN pattern, usually spares forehead, but can be LMN if brainstem – check long tracts)
  - Lyme neuroborreliosis (bilateral palsy, erythema migrans, tick exposure)
  - Guillain–Barré / Miller Fisher (bilateral, areflexia, ophthalmoplegia)
  - Otitis media / mastoiditis (ear pain, fever, tympanic membrane changes)
  - Parotid gland tumour (gradual onset, mass, facial nerve palsy persisting)
  - Sarcoidosis / other granulomatous disease (bilateral, other systemic features)
- **Splitter:** *Isolated forehead involvement (unilateral)?* In Bell’s palsy, forehead is always weak. In UMN stroke, forehead is spared. So if you see facial weakness *with forehead spared*, that is an UMN lesion and is NOT Bell’s palsy – immediate CT head and stroke call.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - FAST / NIHSS stroke screen
  - Otoscopy (check for vesicles, redness, bulging tympanic membrane)
  - Assess eye closure and corneal reflex (if absent, high risk of exposure keratopathy)
  - Pure tone audiometry (if hearing loss or vertigo)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR, U&Es, LFTs, glucose (rule out infection, diabetes as possible precipitant)
  - Serology for Lyme (ELISA + confirmatory Western blot) if unexplained bilateral palsy or tick exposure
  - Varicella-zoster IgG/IgM if suspect Ramsay Hunt (or PCR of vesicle fluid)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None routinely if typical Bell’s palsy (isolated, acute, LMN, no other signs)
  - **Second-line:** CT or MRI head with contrast if atypical features (slow onset, recurrent, other CN involvement, bilateral, no recovery after 4 months) — to exclude mass, meningeal disease, brainstem lesion
- **Special tests:**
  - Electromyography/nerve conduction studies (only if poor recovery or to prognosticate — rarely done acutely)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: no widely accepted scoring system. NICE CKS defines Bell’s palsy as acute (within 72 hours) unilateral LMN facial palsy with no other neurological signs and no identifiable cause.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **FAST / NIHSS stroke screen** – because missing a brainstem stroke (even without limb symptoms) can be catastrophic. If any UMN sign, positive FAST, or facial weakness spares the forehead → head CT.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess eye closure: if incomplete, apply lubricating eye drops (hypromellose 0.3% qds) and eye pad/taping at night to prevent corneal abrasion.
  - Consider if Lyme or Ramsay Hunt is likely (bilateral, rash, tick exposure) – do not start steroids without first ruling out herpes or Lyme (or if high suspicion, add antiviral).
  - Prescribe prednisolone as soon as diagnosis is certain (within 72 hours of onset).
  - Provide patient information leaflet on Bell’s palsy and eye care.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually not admitted unless severe eye involvement, severe pain, unable to eat/drink, or diagnostic uncertainty.
  - If admitted: ensure eye care (lubrication, taping) 4-hourly. Observations NEWS2.
  - Monitor for pain or progression (if worsens or bilateral, reassess for Guillain–Barré).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue prednisolone for 10 days.
  - Eye protection throughout recovery (artificial tears, nocturnal ointment, taping, moisture chamber glasses – consider ophthalmology referral if persistent corneal exposure).
  - Facial exercises (encourage gentle massage and symmetrical movements once acute phase passes) – speech and language therapy referral for severe cases.
  - Follow-up in 2–4 weeks with GP or neurology to assess recovery.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline if nil by mouth, but most patients can swallow. Encourage oral fluids.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely needed (low risk) unless additional immobility.
  - **Diet/NBM:** Normal oral diet if safe swallow; if drooling or dysphagia consider speech and language therapy review.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 q4h if admitted; otherwise, no vital signs required for outpatient.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (for confirmed Bell’s palsy):**
  - **Prednisolone** 50 mg orally once daily for 10 days (or 25 mg twice daily) – start within 72 hours of symptom onset for maximal benefit. [BNF: 50 mg once daily x10 days]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Aciclovir** 800 mg orally 5 times daily for 7 days – add only if Ramsay Hunt syndrome confirmed or suspected (vesicles present) or in severe Bell’s palsy with high suspicion (some clinicians consider adding aciclovir if severe, but NICE does not routinely recommend). Not used alone without steroids.
  - For pregnant women: prednisolone is safe; aciclovir is safe. (Acyclovir for Ramsay Hunt)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Prednisolone: caution in diabetes (monitor glucose), hypertension, active peptic ulcer, severe infection (e.g., herpes simplex keratitis – avoid if ocular HSV suspected)
  - Aciclovir: reduce dose in renal impairment; caution with nephrotoxic drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir dose adjustment in CKD (eGFR <25 mL/min): reduce to 800 mg 3 times daily or [check local policy]
  - Prednisolone: no routine adjustment; may increase risk of fluid retention in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving steroids without ruling out Ramsay Hunt or herpes zoster** – can worsen disseminated VZV. Always examine ear and palate for vesicles before starting prednisolone alone. If vesicles present, add aciclovir.
  - **Omission of eye care** – not a drug error but more harmful: if you discharge without prescribing lubricating eye drops or covering the eye at night, the patient can develop corneal ulceration and permanent vision loss.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Corneal abrasion / exposure keratitis / ulceration (from incomplete eye closure)
  - Syncope or aspiration (rare)
  - Pain
- **Chronic:**
  - Synkinesis (involuntary facial movements – e.g., eye closing when smiling) – occurs in ~10–15% after recovery
  - Facial contracture (long-standing muscle tonicity)
  - Persistent facial weakness (caused by severe degeneration; see prognosis)
  - Psychosocial effects (low mood, social withdrawal)

### Prognosis
- 70–85% recover fully (within 3–6 months). Of the rest, ~15% have mild residual weakness, <5% have severe permanent palsy. Prognosis worse if: complete palsy on presentation, severe pain, older age, hypertension, diabetes, or no improvement within 3 weeks. If recovery starts within 3 weeks, outlook is excellent.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a thorough ear exam** (otorrhea, vesicles, bulging tympanic membrane) – you will not miss Ramsay Hunt or otitis media.
- **Always prescribe eye protection for the first 2 weeks** even if the patient has partial closure – give hypromellose drops 4-hourly and Lacri-Lube ointment at night with tape or patch. Document in notes.
- **Tell the patient to return if they cannot close the eye at all** or develop new neurological symptoms (headache, limb weakness, double vision) – stroke can still be missed initially.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not label facial weakness as Bell’s palsy if forehead is spared** – this is an UMN pattern, treat as a stroke until proven otherwise. Do not discharge without CT head.
- **Do not start steroids if you suspect varicella-zoster** – wait for vesicle examination. If rash present, start aciclovir and prednisolone together (but never steroids alone for Ramsay Hunt).
- **Do not reassure that recovery is guaranteed in 2 weeks** – some take months; warn about synkinesis and need for follow-up if no improvement by 3–4 weeks.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Eye pain, redness, or visual disturbance (corneal ulcer)
  - New facial weakness on opposite side (bilateral suggests Guillain–Barré or Lyme)
  - Severe headache, double vision, or arm/leg weakness (possible stroke)
  - Worsening weakness or failure to improve by 3–4 weeks
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP at 2–4 weeks; if no improvement by 4 weeks, refer to neurology
  - Eye clinic if persistent corneal exposure or risk of ulceration

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior or neurology (on-call) immediately if:**
  - Patient has bilateral facial palsy (suspect Guillain–Barré, Lyme, or sarcoid)
  - Any UMN signs (forehead sparing, hemiparesis, visual field loss)
  - Fever, headache, meningism (consider meningitis or brainstem encephalitis)
  - Rapidly progressive weakness or respiratory muscle involvement (Guillain–Barré — monitor spirometry)
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising (e.g., respiratory distress from aspiration or Guillain–Barré)
  - GCS ≤13
  - Seizure
  - Suspected stroke with evidence of brainstem dysfunction (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular breathing) → airway support before CT
